senses.definition: (intransitive) To move or burst forth.
examples: The boat sprang a leak and began to sink.

quotes.text: ...þe wound þat was springand with huge stremes of blude...
quotes.reference: c. 1540, Livy, translated by John Bellenden, History of Rome, Vol. I, i, xxii, p. 125:

subsenses.definition: To appear.

quotes.text: ...so the man tooke his concubine, and brought her foorth vnto them, and they knew her, and abused her all the night vntil the morning: and when the day began to spring, they let her goe.
quotes.reference: 1611, The Holy Bible, […] (King James Version), London: […] Robert Barker, […], →OCLC, Judges 19:25:

quotes.text:
Home I would go,
 But that my Dores are hatefull to my eyes.
 Fill'd and damm'd up with gaping Creditors,
 Watchfull as Fowlers when their Game will ſpring; […]
quotes.reference: 1682, Thomas Otway, Venice Preserv’d, or, A Plot Discover’d. A Tragedy. […], London: […] Jos[eph] Hindmarsh […], →OCLC, Act I, scene i, page 4:

subsenses.definition: To grow, to sprout.

quotes.text:
Who hath diuided a water-course for the ouerflowing of waters? or a way for the lightning of thunder,
To cause it to raine on the earth, where no man is: on the wildernesse wherein there is no man?
To satisfie the desolate and waste ground, and to cause the bud of the tender herbe to spring forth.
quotes.reference: 1611, The Holy Bible, […] (King James Version), London: […] Robert Barker, […], →OCLC, Job 38:25–27:

quotes.text:
Commerce! beneath whose poison-breathing shade
 No solitary virtue dares to spring, […]
quotes.reference: 1813, Percy Bysshe Shelley, “Canto V”, in Queen Mab; […], London: […] P. B. Shelley, […], →OCLC, page 61:

quotes.text: Dr. Sigmund Freud... says that everything you and I do springs from two motives: the sex urge and the desire to be great.
quotes.reference: 1936, Dale Carnegie, How to Win Friends and Influence People, page 42:

quotes.text: There was moisture in the ground, and from it sprang a million flowers, gold and blue and brown and red.
quotes.reference: 1974, James Albert Michener, Centennial, page 338:

quotes.text: Foxglove sprang tall and purple among the trees.
quotes.reference: 2006, N. Roberts, Morrigann's Cross, section VI:

subsenses.definition: (UK dialectal) To mature.
(figurative) To arise, to come into existence.
(sometimes figurative) To enliven.
examples: He hit the gas and the car sprang to life.

subsenses.definition: (figurative, usually with cardinal adverbs) To move with great speed and energy.
examples: Deer spring with their hind legs, using their front hooves to steady themselves.
...into helle spring...

quotes.text: Ye kynge... sprange out of his chare and resseyuyd them worshipfully.
quotes.reference: 1474, William Caxton, transl., Game and Playe of the Chesse, iii, vii, 141:

quotes.text:
...the Mountain Stag, that springs
From Height to Height, and bounds along the Plains,
Nor has a Master to restrain his Course...
quotes.reference: 1722, Ambrose Philips, The Briton:

quotes.text:
...out on the lawn there arose such a clatter,
I sprang from my bed to see what was the matter.
quotes.reference: 1827, Clement Clarke Moore, (A Visit from St. Nicholas):

quotes.text: However, with the dainty volume my quondam friend sprang into fame. At the same time he cast off the chrysalis of a commonplace existence.
quotes.reference: 1897 December (indicated as 1898), Winston Churchill, chapter I, in The Celebrity: An Episode, New York, N.Y.: The Macmillan Company; London: Macmillan & Co., Ltd., →OCLC:

quotes.text: Thus she advanced; her belly low, almost touching the surface of the ground—a great cat preparing to spring upon its prey.
quotes.reference: 1912, Edgar Rice Burroughs, chapter 5, in Tarzan of the Apes:

quotes.text: Reporters sprang to the conclusion that the speech would make detailed new commitments...
quotes.reference: 2011 April 11, The Atlantic:

subsenses.definition: (usually with from) To be born, descend, or originate from
examples: He sprang from peasant stock.

quotes.text: From this basis, a first-order difference equation for the evolution of capital per worker is found, and the time path of the economy springs from this equation.
quotes.reference: 2008, George McCandless, The ABCs of RBCs, Harvard University Press, page 7:

subsenses.definition: (obsolete) To rise in social position or military rank, to be promoted.

senses.definition: (transitive) To cause to spring (all senses).

subsenses.definition: (of mechanisms) To cause to work or open by sudden application of pressure.
examples: He sprang the trap.

quotes.text: They sprung another Mine... wherein was placed about sixtie Barrels of Powder.
quotes.reference: 1625, Samuel Purchas, Purchas His Pilgrimes, Vol. II, x, ix:

quotes.text: On the 23d, the Besiegers sprung a Mine under the Salient Angle, upon the Right of the Haif Moon, which had the desired Success, the Enemy's Gallery on that Side, and the Mason-Work of the Counterscarp, being thereby demolished.
quotes.reference: 1747, The London Magazine, Or, Gentleman's Monthly Intelligencer:

senses.definition: (transitive) To leap over.

quotes.text: I sprang the fence, and was soon in the village street.
quotes.reference: 1876, Matilda Leathes, Our village worthies; or, Stories of village life, page 112:

senses.definition: (obsolete, of horses) To breed with, to impregnate.

quotes.text: ...[they] sought the fairest stoned horses to spring their mares...
quotes.reference: 1585, Nicolas De Nicolay, translated by Thomas Washington, The Navigations, Peregrinations, and Voyages, Made into Turkie..., Bk. IV, p. 154:

senses.definition: (transitive, obsolete) To wet, to moisten.
(intransitive, now usually with "apart" or "open") To burst into pieces, to explode, to shatter.

quotes.text: On the 22nd the mines sprang, and took very good effect.
quotes.reference: 1698, François Froger, A Relation of a Voyage Made... on the Coasts of Africa, page 30:

senses.definition: (obsolete, military) To go off.

quotes.text: The whole contraption appears liable to spring apart at any moment.
quotes.reference: 2012 April 21, Sydney Morning Herald, page 5:

senses.definition: (ambitransitive, nautical, usually perfective) To crack.
examples: The Edward sprang hir foremast.

senses.definition: To come upon and flush out.

quotes.text: For generations of men the springer spaniel has been looked upon as the dog for springing pheasants in covert and finding and retrieving dead birds or winged runners when ordered to do so. The properly broken dog will not chase, but drop to wing and shot.
quotes.reference: 1921, Field and Stream, page 832:

quotes.text: […] by the beginning of this century a still smaller breed, with a weight of 4 of 5 pounds and a chest measurement of around 12 inches, had come into being for springing rabbits. Such, then, is a rough, quick ancestral picture of our modern Dachshund, and[…]
quotes.reference: 1940, Allen A. Day, “Dachsunds for Woodchucks”, in Dwight Williams Huntington, editor, The Game Breeder and Sportsman, page 94:

quotes.text: I winter, ruffed grouse sometimes roost at night on the ground under the insulating snow. Even during the midday hours, I have often flushed grouse out from under the snow-bowed branches of "buck-brush," the type of environment where a hunter would more likely expect to spring a rabbit or two.
quotes.reference: 2003 August 1, Dennis Walrod, Grouse Hunter's Guide: Solid Facts, Insights, and Observations on How to Hunt Ruffled Grouse, Stackpole Books, →ISBN:

senses.definition: (Australia, slang) To catch in an illegal act or compromising position.

quotes.text: He figured that nobody would ever spring him, but he figured wrong.
quotes.reference: 1980, John Hepworth et al., Boozing Out in Melbourne Pubs..., page 42:

senses.definition: (obsolete) To begin.
(obsolete, slang) To put bad money into circulation.
To tell, to share.
examples: Sorry to spring it on you like this but I've been offered another job.

quotes.text: North Korea loves to spring surprises. More unusual is for its US foe to play along.
quotes.reference: 2012 February 29, Aidan Foster-Carter, “North Korea: The Denuclearisation Dance Resumes”, in BBC News:

senses.definition: (transitive, slang, US) To free from imprisonment, especially by facilitating an illegal escape.
examples: His lieutenants hired a team of miners to help spring him.

quotes.text: If I was in jail I know you'd spring me
quotes.reference: 2007, Mike Batt, “If You Were a Sailboat”, performed by Katie Melua:

senses.definition: (intransitive, slang, rare) To be free of imprisonment, especially by illegal escape.
To secure a person's release from custody; to free or bail out of jail.

quotes.text: The Big Man, of course, had had an alibi as solid as Fort Knox. He had been held and questioned, but was quickly sprung by the best lawyer in Harlem.
quotes.reference: 1954, Ian Fleming, “A Visiting-Card”, in Live and Let Die, London: Pan Books, published 1957, page 22:

senses.definition: (transitive, architecture, of arches) To build, to form the initial curve of.
examples: They sprung an arch over the lintel.

senses.definition: (intransitive, architecture, of arches, with "from") To extend, to curve.
examples: The arches spring from the front posts.

senses.definition: (transitive, nautical) To turn a vessel using a spring attached to its anchor cable.
(transitive) To pay or spend a certain sum, to yield.

quotes.text: He wouldn't spring a nickel for a bag of peanuts.
quotes.reference: 1957, Pelham Grenville Wodehouse, Over Seventy, page 137:

senses.definition: (obsolete, intransitive, slang) To raise an offered price.
(transitive, US, dialectal) Alternative form of sprain.
(transitive, US, dialectal) Alternative form of strain.
(intransitive, obsolete) To act as a spring: to strongly rebound.
(transitive, rare) To equip with springs, especially (of vehicles) to equip with a suspension.
(figurative, rare, obsolete) to inspire, to motivate.
(ambitransitive) To deform owing to excessive pressure, to become warped; to intentionally deform in order to position and then straighten in place.
examples: A piece of timber sometimes springs in seasoning.
He sprang in the slat.

quotes.text: Don't drive it in too hard, as it will ‘spring’ the plane-iron, and make it concave.
quotes.reference: 1873 July, Routledge's Young Gentleman's Magazine, page 503:

senses.definition: (intransitive, UK, dialectal, chiefly of cows) To swell with milk or pregnancy.

quotes.text:
“Gee, Dad, Nancy’s springing all right,” Ray said and paused in spontaneous pleasure.
Stan Parker came, and together they looked at their swelling heifer.
quotes.reference: 1955, Patrick White, chapter 15, in The Tree of Man, New York: Viking, page 228:

senses.definition: (transitive, of rattles, archaic) To sound, to play.

quotes.text: I do not know how John and his mistress would have settled the fate of the thief, but just at this moment a policeman entered — for the cook had sprung the rattle, and had been screaming "Murder" and "Thieves."
quotes.reference: 1850, Samuel Prout Newcombe, Pleasant pages, page 197:

senses.definition: (of animals) To find or get enough food during springtime.
(countable) An act of springing: a leap, a jump.

quotes.reference: 1700, John Dryden, The Cock and the Fox:

senses.definition: (countable, uncountable) The season of the year in temperate regions in which temperatures and daylight hours rise, and plants spring from the ground and into bloom and dormant animals spring to life.
examples: Spring is the time of the year most species reproduce.
You can visit me in the spring, when the weather is bearable.

quotes.text:
No joy the blowing season gives,
⁠The herald melodies of spring,
⁠But in the songs I love to sing
A doubtful gleam of solace lives.
quotes.reference: 1850, [Alfred, Lord Tennyson], “Canto XXXVIII”, in In Memoriam, London: Edward Moxon, […], →OCLC, page 59:

quotes.text: Last spring, the periodical cicadas emerged across eastern North America. Their vast numbers and short above-ground life spans inspired awe and irritation in humans—and made for good meals for birds and small mammals.
quotes.reference: 2012 March-April, Anna Lena Phillips, “Sneaky Silk Moths”, in American Scientist, volume 100, number 2, archived from the original on 19 Feb 2013, page 172:

subsenses.definition: (astronomy) The period from the moment of vernal equinox (around March 21 in the Northern Hemisphere) to the moment of the summer solstice (around June 21); the equivalent periods reckoned in other cultures and calendars.
examples: Chinese New Year always occurs in January or February but is called the "Spring Festival" throughout East Asia because it is reckoned as the beginning of their spring.

subsenses.definition: (meteorology) The three months of March, April, and May in the Northern Hemisphere and September, October, and November in the Southern Hemisphere.
examples: I spent my spring holidays in Morocco.
The spring issue will be out next week.

senses.definition: (uncountable, figurative) The time of something's growth; the early stages of some process.

quotes.text: ...and it came to passe about the spring of the day, that Samuel called Saul to the top of the house...
quotes.reference: 1611, The Holy Bible, […] (King James Version), London: […] Robert Barker, […], →OCLC, 1 Samuel 9:26:

quotes.text:
O how this spring of love resembleth
The uncertain glory of an April day.
quotes.reference: c. 1590–1591 (date written), William Shakespeare, “The Two Gentlemen of Verona”, in Mr. William Shakespeares Comedies, Histories, & Tragedies […] (First Folio), London: […] Isaac Iaggard, and Ed[ward] Blount, published 1623, →OCLC, [Act I, scene iii]:

subsenses.definition: (figurative, politics) a period of political liberalization and democratization
examples: Arab Spring
Prague Spring

senses.definition: (countable, fashion) Someone with ivory or peach skin tone and eyes and hair that are not extremely dark, seen as best suited to certain colors of clothing.
(countable) Something which springs, springs forth, springs up, or springs back, particularly

subsenses.definition: (geology) A spray or body of water springing from the ground.
examples: This beer was brewed with pure spring water.

subsenses.definition: (oceanography, obsolete) The rising of the sea at high tide.
(oceanography) Ellipsis of spring tide, the especially high tide shortly after full and new moons.

An elastic mechanical part or device in any shape (e.g., flat, curved, coiled), made of flexible material (usually spring steel) that exerts force and attempts to spring back when bent, compressed, or stretched.
examples: We jumped so hard the bed springs broke.

subsenses.definition: (nautical) A line from a vessel's end or side to its anchor cable used to diminish or control its movement.

quotes.text: He had warped round with the springs on his cable, and had recommenced his fire upon the Aurora.
quotes.reference: 1836, Frederick Marryat, Mr. Midshipman Easy, volume III, page 72:

subsenses.definition: (nautical) A line laid out from a vessel's end to the opposite end of an adjacent vessel or mooring to diminish or control its movement.
examples: You should put a couple of springs onto the jetty to stop the boat moving so much.

quotes.text: Spring is likewise a rope reaching diagonally from the stern of a ship to the head of another which lies along-side or a-breast of her.
quotes.reference: 1769, William Falconer, An Universal Dictionary of the Marine, s.v:

quotes.text: ‘Springs’ are the ropes used on a ship that is alongside a berth to prevent fore and aft movements.
quotes.reference: 2007 January 26, Business Times::

subsenses.definition: (figurative) A race, a lineage.
(figurative) A youth.
A shoot, a young tree.
A grove of trees; a forest.

senses.definition: (countable, slang) An erection of the penis.
(countable, nautical, obsolete) A crack which has sprung up in a mast, spar, or (rare) a plank or seam.

quotes.text: A spar is said to be sprung, when it is cracked or split,... and the crack is called a spring.
quotes.reference: 1846, Arthur Young, Nautical Dictionary, page 292:

senses.definition: (uncountable) Springiness: an attribute or quality of springing, springing up, or springing back, particularly

subsenses.definition: Elasticity: the property of a body springing back to its original form after compression, stretching, etc.
examples: the spring of a bow

subsenses.definition: Elastic energy, power, or force.
examples:
Heav'ns what a spring was in his Arm, to throw:
How high he held his Shield, and rose at ev'ry blow!

quotes.text: Mrs Durbeyfield, excited by her song, trod the rocker with all the spring that was left in her after a long day's seething in the suds.
quotes.reference: 1891, Thomas Hardy, Tess of the d'Urbervilles, volume 1, London: James R. Osgood, McIlvaine and Co., page 30:

senses.definition: (countable) The source from which an action or supply of something springs.

quotes.text: As wel the singers as the players on instruments shall bee there: all my springs are in thee.
quotes.reference: 1611, The Holy Bible, […] (King James Version), London: […] Robert Barker, […], →OCLC, Psalms 87:7:

quotes.text: Such a man can do all things through Christ that strengtheneth him, he can patiently suffer all things with cheerfull submission and resignation to the Divine Will. He has a secret Spring of spiritual Joy, and the continual Feast of a good Conscience within, that forbid him to be miserable.
quotes.reference: 1693, The Folly and Unreasonableness of Atheism..., Richard Bentley, Sermon 1:

quotes.text: […] discover, at least in some degree, the secret springs and principles, by which the human mind is actuated in its operations?
quotes.reference: 1748, David Hume, Enquiries Concerning the Human Understanding and Concerning the Principles of Morals, London: Oxford University Press, published 1973, §9:

quotes.text:
‘Have you ever contemplated, Adrian, the phenomenon of springs?’
‘Coils, you mean?’
‘Not coils, Adrian, no. Coils not. Think springs of water. Think wells and spas and sources. Well-springs in the widest and loveliest sense. Jerusalem, for instance, is a spring of religiosity. One small town in the desert, but the source of the world’s three most powerful faiths. […] Religion seems to bubble from its sands.’
quotes.reference: 1991 September, Stephen Fry, “[Prelude to chapter 1]”, in The Liar, London: Heinemann, →ISBN, section, page 1:

senses.definition: (countable) Something which causes others or another to spring forth or spring into action, particularly

subsenses.definition: A cause, a motive, etc.

quotes.text: Our Author ſhuns by vulgar Springs to move / The Hero's Glory, or the Virgin's Love; […]
quotes.reference: 1712 (date written), [Alexander] Pope, “Prologue, by Mr. Pope. Spoken by Mr. [Robert] Wilks.”, in [Joseph] Addison, Cato, a Tragedy. […], London: […] J[acob] Tonson, […], published 1713, →OCLC:

subsenses.definition: (obsolete, music) A lively piece of music.

quotes.text:
The firstand spring the fiddle did play
Hey wi the gay and the grinding
Said, "Ye'll drown my sister, as she's dune me."
At the bony bony bows of London
quotes.reference: (Can we date this quote?), stanza 18, in The Bonny Bows o London (as collected by Buchan in 'Ancient ballads and songs of the north of Scotland'):

senses.definition: (intransitive) To spend the springtime somewhere.

quotes.text: True it is that, owing to the migratory propensities of our countrymen, every third man has wintered at Naples, springed at Vienna, summered in Switzerland, and autumned on the banks of the Lago Maggiore;
quotes.reference: 1835 May, “Northern Germany. A Sketch.”, in Fraser’s Magazine for Town and Country, volume XI, number LXV, page 507:

quotes.text: If Tad’s father and Tad had wintered, springed, summered, and autumned together for an hundred years instead of fifteen they could[…]
quotes.reference: 1912, William C[yrus] Sprague, Tad, the Story of a Boy who Had No Chance, page 2:

quotes.text:
They wintered in a warm place
 And summered in a cold,
 But where they springed and autumned
 I never have been told.
quotes.reference: 1937, Mortimer Jones, “Lines of No Importance”, in The Alphi Phi Quarterly, page 29:

quotes.text: She springed in London, summered in Stockholm, autumned at Vichy, and wintered at Monte Carlo.
quotes.reference: 1950, Chambers’s Journal, page 269:

quotes.text: In recent years his friend the fourth-quarter king summered, autumned, and springed in nearby Southern California, which was how they stayed so easily in touch.
quotes.reference: 2006, Tim Pratt, “The Third-Quarter King”, in Jonathan Strahan, Jeremy G Byrne, editors, Eidolon I, →ISBN, page 2:

quotes.text: Larry and Bill had planned to hold a white-linen “fancy” fund-raiser dinner in late June or early July, which would bring out the moneyed crowd who “summered” on the Island. If you summer or winter somewhere you are affluent, Larry knew. (Funny, though, he had never heard of anyone who “autumned” in Vermont or who was “springing” in Colorado.)
quotes.reference: 2010, Larry Stettner, Bill Morrison, Cooking for the Common Good: The Birth of a Natural Foods Soup Kitchen, Berkeley, Calif.: North Atlantic Books, →ISBN, page 84:
